title: Pricing a New Project & Subcontractor Pricing Guide
summary: Erin walked participants through two core pricing tools: the Trade Tracker workflow for managing the bid solicitation process from day one through final quote, and the Subcontractor Pricing Guide for mapping room-by-room finish details before sending pricing requests. The session then opened to Q&A covering budget presentation, overhead and profit line items, project management fees, change order handling, and fixed vs. percentage-based management fees.

teachings.explanation: A structured day-by-day timeline for managing bid solicitation from trades, starting with sending plans on day one, confirming receipt on day three, answering questions on day five, following up on day ten, and requiring quotes by day eleven. The system ensures trades are actively moved through the process rather than left to respond on their own.
teachings.quote: We do not leave it up to them and hope for day five that they have questions. We've got to move them along with us.

teachings.explanation: A room-by-room chart that maps finish details such as flooring, walls, ceilings, millwork, and doors sourced from the architect and designer. It is issued to trades so they are all bidding from the same information baseline, reducing confusion and improving bid accuracy.
teachings.quote: I don't know of anyone in the industry really doing it this way. It's just something that we've kind of reacted to over time so that we can kind of get our heads straight on a project.

teachings.explanation: Erin requires a high degree of client alignment—approximately 95% certainty the builder is the right fit—before investing significant time in the full pricing process. This is often established by walking a completed project with the potential client first.
teachings.quote: If you're like 95% sure that we're the right builder for you, then we'll go ahead and go through this pricing process.

teachings.explanation: When soliciting bids from multiple subcontractors for the same trade, any clarifications, additions, or corrections discovered from one bidder must be communicated to all others so that every bid reflects the same scope of work.
teachings.quote: We want to make sure that everybody's analyzing things consistently. Architects do that with builders, right?

teachings.concept: Separating Overhead and Profit as Distinct Line Items
teachings.explanation: Rather than combining overhead and profit into one fee, Erin separates them so clients can clearly understand what each covers. Overhead funds ongoing business operations; profit ensures the company survives and can stand behind its work long-term. Separation also protects both from client negotiation.
teachings.quote: We make a profit and we're around for the next 10 or 20 years for people. And so we have just been upfront about that.

teachings.concept: Project Management as a Separate Line Item
teachings.explanation: The cost of a field project manager should be broken out as its own line item below the cost-of-work total, separate from the builder's overhead and profit fee. This reflects the distinct role of field management versus back-office operations and is critical for company financial health.
teachings.quote: My business would not have survived if I didn't have this project management cost right here.

teachings.concept: Fixed Management Fee on Cost-Plus Projects
teachings.explanation: One participant shared a hybrid model where the management fee is fixed at contract signing based on the original budget, even though actual costs fluctuate during construction. This removes ongoing fee-related friction with clients and keeps focus on the build rather than the builder's compensation.
teachings.quote: As soon as the focus gets on the builder and how much the builder's making or not making, we're losing track of what we're doing. We're trying to build a beautiful home for them and make it an enjoyable experience.

teachings.explanation: Erin assigns a percent-certainty rating to each cost code in the budget to indicate how confirmed a number is. This transparency allows her to revisit uncertain line items as the project progresses and set proper client expectations about potential price movement.
teachings.quote: That's why in the initial budget, I'm so heavy on percent certainty, because I can always reference, I can always go back, like, well, we actually missed a few details.

actionable_advice: Walk a completed project with potential clients before committing to the full pricing process to confirm fit on both sides.
Send one comprehensive pricing packet to all trades simultaneously, including architectural plans, spec book, designer allowances, engineering documents, and the subcontractor pricing guide.
Call trades on day three to confirm receipt of the bid package rather than assuming the email was seen.
Color-code team members' entries in the trade tracker so it is clear who took which action and when.
When bidding multiple trades for the same scope, distribute all clarifications and additions to every bidder so quotes are comparable.
Hire an independent estimator to do framing takeoffs when lumber yards are too slow to respond within the pricing timeline.
Separate overhead and profit into two distinct line items in the budget presentation to prevent clients from negotiating the combined number down.
Add a project management fee as its own line item below the cost-of-work total on every project budget.
Prepare two to three responses in advance for the most common client questions about fees and overhead before presenting a budget.
Use a miscellaneous and contingency cost code to absorb small field fluctuations without generating formal change orders, reserving paperwork for larger cost-code-level changes.
Have an in-person or direct conversation with clients before any significant budget adjustment is finalized, rather than presenting surprises after money is spent.
client_challenges: Lumber yards and suppliers for engineered wood products often take three to four weeks to return quotes, making the ten-to-eleven-day pricing timeline difficult to meet.
Meeting with a prospective client who has architectural plans but no interior designer yet, making it hard to provide accurate finish-level pricing.
How to structure and communicate the overhead line item on smaller projects where a high combined fee percentage feels difficult to justify to clients.
Uncertainty about whether to charge a project management fee separately when the owner is also serving as the project manager on a small team.
Clients on cost-plus contracts sometimes ask what the builder's incentive is to save money since the builder profits from higher costs.
How to handle situations where a subcontractor's final cost exceeds their original quoted scope—determining who absorbs the overage.
How to update the budget for material price fluctuations during construction and whether that requires formal change orders or can be handled through ongoing budget modifications.
Feeling uncomfortable charging appropriate fees due to fear of losing the work or concern about overcharging clients, even when current pricing results in the builder losing money.
notable_quotes: On qualifying clients before pricing: 'If you're like 95% sure that we're the right builder for you, then we'll go ahead and go through this pricing process. But if we're not, I totally get it.'
On knowing your project better than competitors: 'If clients are talking to two or three builders, there is a 100% chance of you showing up really knowing this project way better than anyone else.'

On charging project management fees: 'Everybody after this meeting, you need to put PM fees in. It is part of being a healthy company, and don't justify—well, I'm the owner and the project manager—don't let them do that to you.'
On phone follow-up with trades: 'You have to pick up the phone and call these trades or you will not get answers. So bottom line.'

On honesty with clients about fees: 'I think people appreciate honesty—literally, people run companies, understand like, here are the numbers. This is my burn rate. This is for me to show up with a smile and make sure my trades show up for you.'

On hustle and fee alignment: 'You're leaving money on the table for the hustle. The hustle factor needs to go up. You can do that. You need to tell people these things—I will hustle for you, here's the percent you get for the hustle.'
